items.title: McBurney sign
items.contentHtml:
McBurney's sign

  • McBurney's sign: maximum pain on palpation at McBurney's point (union of the external third with the internal two thirds of the line that goes from the right anterior superior iliac spine to the umbilicus).
  • Indicates localized peritoneal irritation due to an inflamed appendix in a typical position.
  • Sensitivity limited (~50-85%), but high specificity if positive.
  • In retrocecal or pelvic appendicitis, the McBurney sign may be absent.

items.title: Alvarado Scale (MANTRELS)
items.contentHtml:
Alvarado Scale

  • Scores 10 points: pain migration (1), anorexia (1), nausea/vomiting (1), pain in FID (2), rebound (1), thermal elevation >37.3°C (1), leukocytosis >10,000 (2), left deviation >75% (1).
  • Threshold: ≥7 points indicates high probability of appendicitis; ≤4 points low probability.
  • Does not replace the clinic or the image; useful for stratification in emergencies.
  • In children under 12 years of age or pregnant women, the Alvarado Scale loses precision.

items.title: Pediatric appendicitis
items.contentHtml:
Appendicitis in children

  • Atypical presentation: frequent vomiting, high fever, initial diffuse pain; more difficult diagnosis.
  • Modified Alvarado Scale (Pediatric Appendicitis Score) uses cough sign instead of rebound.
  • First line diagnosis: abdominal ultrasound to avoid radiation; if doubtful, CT with low dose.
  • Higher perforation rate (30-50%) due to diagnostic delay; Urgent appendectomy and antibiotic coverage with piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone + metronidazole.

items.title: Geriatric appendicitis
items.contentHtml:
Appendicitis in the elderly

  • Subtle symptoms: mild pain, absence of fever or leukocytosis in 30-40% of cases.
  • High clinical suspicion; CT is the study of choice due to its sensitivity >95%.
  • Comorbidities and polypharmacy increase the risk of complications; perforation up to 50% on admission.
  • Management: urgent appendectomy (laparoscopic if possible) plus broad-spectrum antibiotics (eg ertapenem).

items.title: CT in appendicitis
items.contentHtml:
Diagnostic imaging (CT)

  • CT with IV contrast is the gold standard: sensitivity 94-98%, specificity 95-99%.
  • Key findings: appendix >7 mm in diameter, wall thickening, mural enhancement, periappendiceal fat infiltration, free fluid or abscess.
  • “Target” sign or “double halo” indicates transmural inflammation.
  • In children and pregnant women, ultrasound or MRI is preferred to avoid radiation; If CT is necessary, use low-dose protocol.

items.title: Appendiceal perforation
items.contentHtml:
Complication: perforation

  • Occurs in 15-30% of appendicitis, more common in elderly and children.
  • Clinics: generalized pain, high fever, peritonitis, paralytic ileus; Blumberg sign (rebound pain) positive.
  • Image: CT shows extraluminal air, periappendiceal abscess or plastron.
  • Management: urgent appendectomy plus peritoneal lavage; antibiotics for 7-14 days (eg ceftriaxone + metronidazole).

items.title: Rovsing maneuver
items.contentHtml:
Rovsing maneuver

  • Rovsing maneuver: pain in the right iliac fossa when palpating the left iliac fossa (compression of the descending colon displaces gas towards the cecum, distending the inflamed appendix).
  • Low sensitivity (~40-60%), but very specific (>90%) when positive.
  • Indicates irritation localized peritoneal; It does not rule out appendicitis if it is negative.
  • In retrocecal or pelvic appendicitis, the Rovsing Maneuver is usually negative.

items.title: Psoas and obturator signs
items.contentHtml:
Psoas and obturator

  • Psoas sign: pain with passive extension of the right hip (patient in left lateral decubitus position). Indicates irritation of the psoas muscle due to the retrocecal appendix.
  • Obturator sign: pain with passive internal rotation of the flexed right hip. It suggests a pelvic appendix close to the obturator internus muscle.
  • Both signs have specificity >85% but limited sensitivity (~30-50%).
  • Its positivity guides the anatomical position of the appendix and helps in the surgical decision.

items.title: urgent appendectomy
items.contentHtml:
Urgent surgical treatment

  • Appendectomy (open or laparoscopic) is the definitive treatment; should be performed within the first 24 hours of diagnosis.
  • Delay >24-48 hours increases the risk of perforation and complications.
  • In uncomplicated appendicitis, laparoscopic appendectomy is the technique of choice due to less postoperative pain and hospital stay.
  • In cases of plastron or abscess, initial conservative management (antibiotics + drainage) and delayed appendectomy may be chosen. 6-8 weeks.

items.title: Antibiotic prophylaxis
items.contentHtml:
Preoperative antibiotic

  • Administer 30-60 minutes before the surgical incision.
  • Regimen of choice: cefazolin 2 g IV (or ceftriaxone 1 g IV) plus metronidazole 500 mg IV to cover gram-negative and anaerobes.
  • In those allergic to beta-lactams: clindamycin 600 mg IV + gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV or levofloxacin 500 mg IV + metronidazole.
  • If there is perforation or peritonitis, continue antibiotics for 3-5 postoperative days.

items.title: Laparoscopic appendectomy
items.contentHtml:
Laparoscopic appendectomy

  • Gold standard technique in uncomplicated appendicitis: less pain, shorter hospital stay, faster return to work.
  • Relative contraindications: generalized peritonitis, advanced pregnancy (third trimester), uncorrected coagulopathy.
  • 3 ports are used (umbilical, suprapubic, left flank); The mesoappendix is ​​sectioned with a harmonic scalpel or vascular ligation.
  • In complicated appendicitis (perforation, abscess), laparoscopic appendectomy is safe if the surgeon is experienced; requires conversion to open in 5-10% of cases.

items.title: Differential diagnosis in women
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis in women

  • In women of childbearing age, rule out gynecological pathology: pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), ruptured ovarian cyst, ectopic pregnancy, ovarian torsion.
  • Perform pelvic ultrasound and pregnancy test (β-hCG) before surgery.
  • EPI presents with bilateral pain, vaginal discharge, fever and pain on cervical mobilization.
  • Ovarian torsion presents with abrupt pain, nausea and adnexal mass; Doppler ultrasound is diagnostic.
